data.title: Orchestra BioMed rises as trial for antihypertensive drug expands
data.description:
Orchestra BioMed (NASDAQ:OBIO) traded higher on Friday after the company announced an FDA-approved protocol update that is set to expand its BACKBEAT study, which evaluates its lead asset AVIM therapy for hypertension in pacemaker-indicated patients.

BACKBEAT is Orchestra's (NASDAQ:OBIO) pivotal trial, designed to evaluate AVIM therapy in patients with uncontrolled hypertension who are receiving antihypertensive drugs and also require a pacemaker.

The update will lead to a 24-fold increase in the patient population eligible for participation in BACKBEAT compared to the original protocol, the New Hope, Pennsylvania-based health equipment developer said.

The company has implemented the revised protocol in Q3 2025 ahead of a full rollout later this year as it seeks to complete the study’s enrollments in mid-2026.

“We believe these updates reinforce the relevance and potential impact of the BACKBEAT study as well as better position us to complete the BACKBEAT study in a timely manner,” CEO David Hochman said.

data.title: Gilead Sciences jumps after Q2 beat; Truist upgrades to Buy
data.description:
Gilead Sciences (NASDAQ:GILD) was upgraded to Buy from Hold at Truist after the company raised full year sales and profit outlook, and reported upbeat Q2 earnings.

The California based biotech raised its full year adjusted profit to a range between $7.95 per share and $8.25 per share, as demand for its HIV products showed robust growth.

"We continue to think GILD has the bestin-class, unparalleled HIV business in the sector. Trodelvy returned to growth this quarter, and more importantly, we are optimistic of frontline uptake in 2026." analysts at Truist said.

Truist also raised its price target for the company to $127 from $108. The research firm added that Gilead's cell therapy continues to face challenges, but it will be invigorated with Anito-cel.

The stock is up about 8.3% so far today. YTD, GILD is up 29.4%.

data.title: American Strategic signals asset sales to unlock capital and reduce debt burden while maintaining 82% occupancy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Strategic Investment Co. (NYC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Nicholas S. Schorsch reported that "our second quarter results were largely in line with the first quarter with occupancy flat at 82% and the continued focus on leasing up available space." He emphasized ongoing efforts to "operate and create value at our current assets with a focus on tenant retention, property improvements and cost efficiency."
  • Schorsch highlighted that "during the quarter, we executed 2 lease renewals, 1 each at 123 William and 1140 Avenue of the Americas, extending the weighted average remaining lease term of the portfolio to 6 years at quarter end." He noted that near-term lease expirations declined to 7% of annualized straight-line rent from 12% last quarter, while 54% of leases now extend beyond 2030, up from 51%.
  • The CEO stated that the company "is continuing to market 123 William Street and 196 Orchard for sale" and expects to use proceeds to retire debt and reinvest in higher-yielding assets.
  • Schorsch described the portfolio as "$440 million, approximately 1 million square foot New York City real estate portfolio... located primarily in Manhattan" with "6 office and retail properties" and "a strong tenant base that includes large investment-grade firms."
  • CFO Michael LeSanto stated, "Second quarter 2025 revenue was $12.2 million compared to $15.8 million in the second quarter of 2024, principally due to the sale of 9 Times Square in the fourth quarter of 2024. The company's GAAP net loss attributable to common stockholders was $41.7 million in the second quarter of 2025 compared to a net loss of $91.9 million in the second quarter of 2024."
  • LeSanto added, "For the second quarter of 2025, adjusted EBITDA was $0.4 million compared to $4.5 million in the second quarter of 2024. Cash net operating income was $4.2 million compared to $7.4 million in the second quarter of 2024."
  • LeSanto disclosed, "during the second quarter, the lender for 1140 Avenue of the Americas commenced foreclosure proceedings with respect to the property."
  • Schorsch reiterated that the ongoing sale process "represents a deliberate strategy designed to unlock substantial capital while reducing our debt burden."

Outlook

  • Schorsch stated that if the sales of 123 William Street and 196 Orchard are consummated on anticipated terms, "we expect to use the net proceeds to retire debt and reinvest in higher-yielding assets to enhance our long-term portfolio value."
  • Management noted that the repositioning is "fundamental to maximizing shareholder returns" and committed to providing "regular updates on our progress."
  • No explicit changes in quantitative forward guidance were provided compared to the previous quarter.

Financial Results

  • LeSanto reported revenue for Q2 2025 at $12.2 million. The GAAP net loss attributable to common stockholders was $41.7 million, which he explained was due primarily to an impairment related to the sale of 9 Times Square.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $0.4 million and cash net operating income was $4.2 million.
  • There was no explicit update on leverage, interest rates, or debt maturity compared to Q1 2025.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: The transcript does not include a question-and-answer session or analyst questions and management responses for the current quarter.

Sentiment Analysis

  • There is no evidence of analyst sentiment in the current quarter transcript due to the absence of a Q&A session.
  • Management maintained a neutral tone in prepared remarks, focusing on current leasing performance, asset sales, and financial stability. Schorsch presented stability in the portfolio and described the asset sale process as "deliberate" and "fundamental to maximizing shareholder returns."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remains consistent, with management continuing to emphasize stability and the strategic rationale for asset sales. There is no shift toward either increased optimism or caution in the language used.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Occupancy remained flat at 82% versus Q1 2025, as stated by Schorsch.
  • The company reduced near-term lease expirations to 7% of annualized straight-line rent from 12% in Q1, and increased the portion of leases extending beyond 2030 from 51% to 54%.
  • The focus on selling 123 William Street and 196 Orchard and using proceeds to reduce debt and reinvest in higher-yielding assets was reiterated, showing continuity in strategic direction.
  • Portfolio valuation declined to $440 million from $488 million in Q1 2025, reflecting asset sales.
  • The lender for 1140 Avenue of the Americas commenced foreclosure proceedings in Q2, a development not present in Q1.
  • Management's tone and messaging remained steady, emphasizing operational stability, cost efficiency, and strategic asset sales.

Risks and Concerns

  • LeSanto reported that "the lender for 1140 Avenue of the Americas commenced foreclosure proceedings with respect to the property," signaling a specific risk to the portfolio.
  • The CEO acknowledged reliance on asset sales to unlock capital and reduce debt, indicating a dependency on successful transactions for strategic repositioning.
  • There is no mention of additional mitigation strategies beyond the reinvestment of sale proceeds and ongoing leasing efforts.

Final Takeaway

Management at American Strategic Investment Co. emphasized portfolio stability through steady occupancy, long-term leases, and a focus on tenant quality. The ongoing marketing of key Manhattan assets for sale, with plans to use proceeds for debt reduction and reinvestment, remains central to the company’s strategy. A notable new risk emerged with foreclosure proceedings at 1140 Avenue of the Americas, while financial results reflect the impact of prior asset sales. The leadership continues to frame these actions as deliberate steps to maximize shareholder value and enhance portfolio returns, maintaining a consistent and measured tone throughout the quarter.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Peloton's path forward gains a bull at Goldman Sachs
data.description:
Peloton Interactive (NASDAQ:PTON) was back in the black last quarter as cuts to its marketing and R&D budgets offset declining memberships and net revenue.

And while the company projected another drop in revenue for FY26, margins will continue to improve, amplified by a cost restructuring plan which intends to achieve at least $100M of run-rate savings by the end of next year.

This earned Peloton (NASDAQ:PTON) an upgrade at Goldman Sachs, which now views the stock as a Buy with a price target of $11.50 (from $7), representing 62% upside from Thursday’s close.

“We see the scope for a return to revenue growth in the middle of FY26 that might then compound at a rate/velocity above our current forward estimate curve, putting Peloton in a solid revision cycle for the next 12–18 months,” Goldman Sachs analyst Eric Sheridan said in his note to clients.

Sheridan’s upbeat outlook for Peloton (NASDAQ:PTON) is not shared by many Seeking Alpha authors, who share a consensus Sell rating on the stock. The bearish view is attributed to the company’s rising debt, interest expenses, and share dilution, which presents “significant financial risks to shareholders,” says SA analyst Manuel Paul Dipold.

“While some margin metrics are improving due to cost savings, the key metrics (equipment sold and subscription revenue) are either declining or stagnant at best. As long as these figures do not improve, there is little hope for increasing revenue and profitability,” Dipold warns.

Goldman’s Sheridan acknowledges that declining revenue through the first quarter of 2026 is a negative for the stock, and caution remains around potential tariff implications. But these could be mitigated by AI personalization and increased partnership relationships to drive growth. In addition, Peloton’s (PTON) results saw elements of improved monetization of the subscriber base.

“We see Peloton as a story with new management, new initiatives aimed at platform growth and monetization for the next few years and a scope for higher incremental returns on capital in the form of free cash flow conversion,” Sheridan says, adding that as investors receive more finite details of how these strategies will be deployed and then executed against “will likely be an element that could build investor confidence in the coming 12–18 months.”

While Seeking Alpha authors view Peloton (PTON) as a Sell, Wall Street analysts see the stock as a Buy. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating splits the difference with a Hold on Peloton (PTON), assigning a Quant score of 3.20 out of 5.

data.title: 10 stocks return 80% of S&P 500’s gains since ‘Liberation Day’
data.description:
Just 10 big stocks encompass about 80% of the S&P 500 (SP500) since Liberation Day.

Those include the Magnificent 7 stocks, plus Broadcom (AVGO), Oracle (ORCL), and Palantir (PLTR), according to BofA Securities.

  • Palantir (PLTR) – Opened 82.40 on April 2, up 127.4%.
  • Broadcom (AVGO) – Opened 166.08 on April 2, up 84%.
  • Oracle (ORCL) – Opened 139.86 on April 2, up 78%.
  • Nvidia (NVDA) – Opened 107.29 on April 2, up 70.5%.
  • Microsoft (MSFT) – Opened 377.97 on April 2, up 37.8%.
  • Meta Platforms (META) – Opened 574.91 on April 2, up 33.1%.
  • Tesla (TSLA) – Opened 254.60 on April 2, up 30.9%
  • Alphabet (GOOGL) – Opened 155.15 on April 2, up 30.4% since then.
  • Amazon (AMZN) – Opened 187.66 on April 2, up 19%.
  • Apple (AAPL) – Opened 221.32 on April 2, up 1.2%.

The S&P 500 (SP500) opened 5,580.76 on Liberation Day, and to date it has advanced 14.3%.

This is a U.S. stock concentration, “but reinforced by pro-monopoly American First policy,” said Michael Hartnett, chief investment strategist at BofA.

data.title: Alpha Metallurgical Resources lowers 2025 cost guidance to $101–$107 per ton while restarting share buybacks amid market volatility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alpha Metallurgical Resources (AMR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Charles Andrew Eidson highlighted "adjusted EBITDA of $46.1 million and 3.9 million tons shipped in the quarter." Eidson noted the company achieved its "best cost performance for the company since 2021," with cost of coal sales down by more than $10 per ton versus the first quarter. He announced that "we have lowered cost guidance for the year, along with additional adjustments to our 2025 expectations for SG&A, net cash interest income and idle operations expense." Eidson also reported that "we ended the second quarter with $557 million in total liquidity, nearly 15% higher than at the end of the first quarter." He revealed, "the Board's decision to restart the buyback program on an opportunistic basis," after five inactive quarters.

  • CFO J. Todd Munsey stated, "Adjusted EBITDA for the second quarter was $46.1 million, up from $5.7 million in the first quarter. We sold 3.9 million tons in Q2, up from 3.8 million tons sold in Q1." Munsey added, "Cost of coal sales for our met segment decreased to $100.06 per ton in the second quarter, down from $110.34 per ton in Q1. Increased productivity, lower labor cost and reduced repair and maintenance expenditures were the primary drivers of the decrease in costs." He provided new guidance: "We are lowering our cost of coal sale guidance for the year to a range of $101 per ton to $107 per ton, down from the prior range of $103 to $110 per ton." Munsey also previewed the potential impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, estimating "the cash benefit of the tax credit may be in the range of $30 million to $50 million annually."

  • President & COO Jason E. Whitehead reported "a 10% increase over Q1 in tons per man hour contributed to lower labor and other fixed costs, and the teams achieved these efficiency gains while reducing supply and maintenance expenses." He confirmed that "the slope development is now approaching 1,625 feet, approximately 93% of the way from the surface to the coal horizon" for the Kingston Wildcat mine, with first coal production expected later this year.

Outlook

  • The company updated its cost of coal sales guidance for 2025 to a range of $101–$107 per ton, down from $103–$110 per ton. SG&A guidance for 2025 was reduced to $48 million–$54 million from the previous $53 million–$59 million range. Idle operations expense guidance increased to $21 million–$29 million (previously $18 million–$28 million). Net cash interest income guidance increased to $6 million–$12 million (from $2 million–$10 million). At the midpoint of guidance, 69% of metallurgical tonnage in the met segment is committed and priced at an average price of $127.37. The thermal byproduct portion is fully committed and priced at $80.52 per ton.

Financial Results

  • Metallurgical segment realizations increased with an average realization of $119.43 per ton in Q2. Export met tons priced against Atlantic indices realized $113.82 per ton, while those on Australian indices realized $109.75 per ton. The weighted average for metallurgical sales was $122.84 per ton. Cost of coal sales for the met segment decreased to $100.06 per ton. SG&A (excluding noncash stock compensation and nonrecurring items) decreased to $11.9 million. CapEx for the quarter was $34.6 million. Liquidity stood at $556.9 million at quarter end. Cash provided by operating activities was $53.2 million in Q2.

Q&A

  • Nicholas Giles, B. Riley Securities: "Can you walk us through where the savings came from?...can you speak to the sustainability of these costs?" Charles Andrew Eidson: "Some of this has been mean reversion...the higher productivity, getting more tons out, increasing the denominator, that was a force multiplier...The changes we've made are kind of fundamental...we're certainly hopeful that we can maintain this run rate."
  • Giles: "Is it fair to assume that we could see 2026 costs dip below the $100 mark?" Eidson: "We missed sub-$100 by $0.07. So is it possible? Certainly, it's possible."
  • Giles: "How are you approaching domestic contracting?" Daniel E. Horn: "We need pricing that works for us over 12 months...we need pricing that sustains us next year regardless of what the seaborne market does."
  • Giles: "In Q2, there was almost a swap sequentially in volume terms...Any color you can add on that?" Horn: "It's not unusual for us to have heavier shipments to Asia...It's up to the buyer's schedule."
  • Nathan Martin, The Benchmark Company: "The updated cash cost guidance for the full year, what net price are you assuming in the back half of the year?" Eidson: "We're just kind of holding flat with where we are."
  • Martin: "How could recent escalation in trade tensions and tariffs impact your business?" Horn: "We haven't had any pushback or negative feedback...business as usual."
  • Martin: "How many domestic tons...contracted for 2025?" Horn: "Somewhere around 3.5 million tons, plus or minus that we'll ship."
  • Martin: "DTA project spending and timeline?" Eidson: "That's about the same cadence...repairs and enhancements should be finished by 2028."
  • Martin: "Union Pacific, Norfolk Southern merger impact?" Eidson: "We'll just have to wait and see on that as well."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive tone, especially regarding the cost improvements, with Giles stating, "your cost improvements quarter-on-quarter were astounding."
  • Management displayed confidence in cost controls and operational progress, using phrases like "we're certainly hopeful that we can maintain this run rate" and "we are proud of achieving our best cost performance in years."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed a more positive and optimistic sentiment, reflecting improved operational results and financial positioning.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company shifted from a focus on weather-driven challenges and cost pressures in Q1 to highlighting operational improvements and cost reductions in Q2.
  • Adjusted EBITDA increased significantly from $5.7 million in Q1 to $46.1 million in Q2.
  • Cost of coal sales dropped substantially, and cost guidance was lowered for the year.
  • SG&A and CapEx both declined quarter-over-quarter.
  • Liquidity rose from $485.8 million to $556.9 million.
  • Management’s tone shifted from caution and defensive actions to cautious optimism and readiness to capitalize on market improvements.
  • Analysts’ focus moved from cost containment and downside risk to sustainability of improvements and forward-looking contracting.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing weak steel demand, global economic growth uncertainty, and trade tensions as persistent headwinds.
  • Met coal indexes reached multi-year lows in some categories, and market volatility remains a risk.
  • Management emphasized ongoing cost controls and liquidity strengthening as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Alpha Metallurgical Resources emphasized substantial quarter-over-quarter cost improvements and a strengthened liquidity position, prompting a reduction in annual cost guidance and the resumption of its share buyback program. Management remains focused on operational discipline and cautious in its market outlook, highlighting preparedness to navigate continued volatility and uncertainty in the metallurgical coal market while advancing key projects like Kingston Wildcat to drive future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Scripps outlines portfolio optimization and debt reduction strategy amid regulatory shifts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The E.W. Scripps Company (SSP) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Adam P. Symson highlighted the completion of the broadcast industry's first station swap transaction, a new multiyear WNBA agreement, and the addition of a fourth NHL team for Scripps Sports, stating, "We delivered a very solid financial performance for the second quarter in the face of a soft advertising climate. That included 900 basis points of Scripps Networks margin improvement." Symson emphasized that these efforts have resulted in a significant reduction of the debt leverage ratio and that "the 2 major refinancing transactions this year have taken care of all of our major short-term maturities."
  • Symson described regulatory changes as a catalyst for growth, saying, "Scripps will be able to go deeper in its local coverage with 3 new duopolies in the Mountain West region. The improved economics will allow us to grow our commitment to quality news gathering and other local programming like sports for the communities we serve."
  • CFO Jason P. Combs reported, "We closed on the placement of $750 million of new senior secured second-lien notes... We have now used the proceeds of this offering to pay off the company's outstanding senior notes that were set to mature in 2027, prepay a portion of our Term Loan B-2 that is due in 2028 and pay off a portion of our revolving credit facilities, leaving $36 million remaining."

Outlook

  • Management expects Q3 Local Media division revenue to be down in the mid- to high 20% range, with core revenue about flat and expenses down in the low to mid-single-digit percent range.
  • For Scripps Networks, revenue is expected to be down low single digits, and expenses down in the mid-single-digit range for Q3.
  • Combs updated full-year guidance, stating, "We now expect our cash interest paid to be between $170 million and $175 million, we expect CapEx to be $45 million to $50 million, and we expect cash taxes paid to be $5 million to $10 million. All of these adjustments will drive incremental cash flow this year."

Financial Results

  • Scripps Networks revenue for Q2 was $206 million. Segment profit was $56 million, with a segment margin of 27%, up from 18% in Q2 2024.
  • Local Media segment profit was nearly $56 million, compared to $88 million in Q2 2024.
  • CTV revenue for the quarter was up 57%.
  • Second quarter EPS was a loss of ($0.59). The quarter included $38 million of financing transaction costs, a $31 million gain on the sale of the West Palm Beach station building, a $5.6 million write-off of deferred financing costs, and a $3 million loss on extinguishment of debt. These items increased the loss by $0.13 per share. The preferred stock dividend reduced EPS by $0.18 this quarter.
  • Cash and cash equivalents at June 30 totaled $31.7 million. Net leverage at the end of Q2 was 4.4x, a half turn below the end of Q1.

Q&A

  • Daniel Louis Kurnos, The Benchmark Company: Asked about the trajectory and targets for further portfolio optimization amid deregulation. Symson responded that the company is "going to do everything in our power to take advantage of this moment," with swaps and select asset sales targeted for portfolio improvement and accelerated debt paydown.
  • Kurnos followed up on Q3 guidance, to which Combs clarified, "political in sort of this odd year is going to be similar to past odd years, kind of mid $20-ish million. It will build throughout the year with the majority of it hitting in Q4."
  • Michael A. Kupinski, NOBLE Capital Markets: Probed the core advertising environment and advertiser sentiment. Combs stated, "We came in down 2% versus the prior year, that's sort of best-in-class from what I've seen across the industry and certainly as it ties directly back to our sports strategy."
  • Kupinski inquired about the impact of declining search traffic. Symson noted, "We do employ a number of different technical solutions to retain the value of our IP and block chatbots from scraping our websites... I don't necessarily think that the integration of AI into core search is going to be a net positive for publishers."
  • Craig Anthony Huber, Huber Research Partners, asked about CBS nonrenewal events and retrans sub losses. Symson described the CBS Atlanta change as "more of a one-off" and reiterated expectations for net retrans margin expansion. Combs added, "We continue to see sort of a down mid-single-digit 12-month trend on our sub churn."
  • Steven Lee Cahall, Wells Fargo, asked about structural ad pressures. Symson said, "The first one is our sports strategy... It's allowing us both through upfront and scatter to ensure we're not just selling that time period but we're selling across network, we're selling CTV."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a cautious but constructive tone, probing for more clarity on guidance, advertiser sentiment, and regulatory timing. Questions reflected concerns about the pace of regulatory change, advertising headwinds, and the durability of sports-driven results.
  • Management maintained a confident stance in prepared remarks but acknowledged macroeconomic uncertainty, with Symson stating, "uncertainty is nobody's friend," and Combs noting "hesitancy is the right word to throw out there for advertisers." The tone was slightly more defensive in the Q&A compared to prepared remarks as they addressed persistent questions on advertising and regulatory timing.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence remained steady on portfolio optimization and debt reduction, but there was increased focus on navigating a soft advertising environment and macroeconomic unpredictability. Analyst tone was more pressing on near-term visibility and the sustainability of sports-driven growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management introduced a new station swap transaction and reported further progress in refinancing and debt retirement, compared to Q1’s focus on refinancing completions and margin expansion.
  • Q2 guidance for Local Media revenue was more negative, with sharper declines expected compared to Q1’s high single-digit down outlook. Networks revenue turned from a flat guide in Q1 to a low single-digit decline for Q3.
  • Scripps Networks segment margin improved from 18% in Q2 2024 to 27% in Q2 2025, while management highlighted a 900 basis point lift.
  • Analyst questions shifted from focus on refinancing and sports asset expansion in Q1 to deeper concern about ad market weakness, regulatory developments, and portfolio optimization in Q2.
  • Management’s tone was slightly more measured regarding advertising recovery, compared to the optimism in Q1 around sports-driven outperformance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited persistent macroeconomic uncertainty, advertising market hesitancy, and automotive category weakness as key challenges.
  • Regulatory outcomes remain pending, particularly regarding FCC ownership rules and national cap changes, with Symson stating, "it's just going to take a little bit of time."
  • Analyst questions identified the risk of further declines in core advertising and the potential impact of declining search referrals and AI on digital monetization.
  • Sub churn in retransmissions continues at a mid-single-digit rate.

Final Takeaway

The E.W. Scripps Company emphasized a disciplined approach to portfolio optimization and debt reduction, leveraging regulatory changes and its sports strategy to navigate a challenging advertising environment. Management signaled confidence in improving cash flow, margin expansion in networks, and further debt paydown, while acknowledging ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and a cautious advertising market. The company remains focused on seizing opportunities presented by regulatory shifts to strengthen its local and national footprint and drive value for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Microchip dips on Q2 results as analysts highlight improving margins
data.description:
Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP) was down 4% during Friday market action following second quarter results that surpassed estimates and demonstrated improving gross margins.

Despite the share drop, analysts were impressed with the results, outlook and improving margins.

"Yesterday, Microchip posted good results with guidance above seasonality, but we believe it was below elevated expectations, which caused the stock to sell off," said Citi analyst Christopher Danely, in an investor note. "We believe all the upturn levers are still there for MCHP with 80% incremental gross margins and some of the highest revenue growth among peers, and we continue to expect MCHP to exhibit the highest EPS growth."

Citi reiterated its Buy rating and $90 price target on the Arizona-based semiconductor firm.

KeyBanc maintained its Overweight rating and $85 price target following the results.

"Mgmt expressed confidence in continued above seasonal revenue growth and improving cycle dynamics, highlighting: 1) decreasing inventory at disty and direct customers; 2) the sell-in/sell-through gap decreased from $103M last qtr to $49M in F1Q; and 3) backlog for F2Q is meaningfully higher than F1Q," said KeyBanc analysts John Vinh and Ryan Rosumny, in an investor note.

Meanwhile, Stifel maintained its Buy rating and $82 price target. Stifel also increased its full-year 2026 earnings per share estimate to $1.47 from $1.35 and increased its 2026 revenue estimate to $4.6B from $4.56B.

"Overall, we were impressed with MCHP's Jun Q print and Sep Q guide, and accordingly we are raising our estimates to reflect the positive dynamics discussed on the call," said Stifel analysts, led by Tore Svanberg, in a note. "While tariff uncertainty continues to weigh on companies across our coverage universe in the NT, we remind investors that MCHP has already progressed through a significant NT correction (revenues declined -44.3% y/y in CY24A) and, even with our raised estimates, we are modeling CY25E to decline -8.8% y/y, which we believe appropriately balances these risks with the company-specific momentum arising from MCHP's Nine Point Recovery Plan."

Microchip's peers such as Texas Instruments (TXN) and STMicroelectronics (STM) were up 0.5% and 2%, respectively.

data.title: Plug Power Q2 results on deck: Here's what to expect
data.description:
Analysts see Plug Power’s (NASDAQ:PLUG) second-quarter results, which is scheduled for August 11, after market close, posting a solid year-on-year jump.

While the company is expected to continue incurring losses, PLUG may trim it down by over 58% compared to last year to -$0.15. Revenue is likely to come in at $157.97M, translating to a 10.2% jump compared to the corresponding quarter last year.

This quarter, investors are likely to focus on how the company’s growth initiatives and strategic partnerships are contributing to revenue and profitability.

Last month, Plug Power announced a new multi-year enhanced supply agreement with "a leading U.S.-based industrial gas company and longtime hydrogen partner. In June, the company expanded its partnership with Allied Green Ammonia with a new 2-electrolyzer opportunity linked to a sustainable fuels project in Uzbekistan.

In Q1, the company posted mixed results with revenue beating estimates but EPS lagging behind. The company maintained its Q2 revenue outlook in the range of $140 million to $180 million. Additionally, the company anticipates further improvement in gross margin and working capital performance from Q1 2025 onward, continuing through the rest of the year.

The management maintained the target of becoming gross margin break-even by the end of the year.

Over the last 3 months, PLUG’s EPS estimates have seen 4 upward revisions and 2 downward moves, while revenue estimates have seen 8 upward revisions and 9 downward moves.
